title: Understanding the Impact of Reviews on Consumer Product Choices Under Negotiated Pricing
abstract: We study the impact of reviews on product choices when consumers can negotiate price. Although prior research has examined the impact of reviews in various contexts, none has considered a setting in which the purchase price is negotiated. We postulate that online reviews affect demand through consumer baseline product preference and consumer bargaining power. Leveraging a unique data set of consumer reviews of new car purchases, we developed and estimated a structural model to decompose and quantify the two mechanisms of how reviews affect demand: baseline preference mechanism versus bargaining power mechanism. Our counterfactual analysis suggests that the bargaining power mechanism is more important than the baseline preference mechanism in explaining how historical price and discount frequency information in reviews affect seller profits. Ignoring the impact of reviews on the negotiated price leads to a biased estimation of the effectiveness of reviews. We call into caution a popular selling practice of using discounts in exchange for positive reviews, as it can backfire when future consumers leverage this discount information to negotiate a lower price.

author.Jisu Cao: Department of Information Systems, W. P. Carey School of Business, Arizona State University, Tempe, Arizona 85281;
author.Sha Yang: Marshall School of Business, University of Southern California, Los Angeles, California 90089

article.1. Introduction: Online reviews have transformed the way consumers make decisions: from researching a new car purchase to seeking a job. A large body of work has documented the link between online reviews and consumer demand. For example,Chevalier and Mayzlin (2006)examine how consumer reviews affect book sales;Liu (2006)studies such an impact on movies’ box office revenue;Zhu and Zhang (2010)look into the video game industry. Although empirical context varies by study, most involve a fixed-price setting and have not explored how online reviews affect product choice when purchase price is negotiable. Price can be negotiated in many settings ranging from goods (e.g., automobiles, electronic appliances, and home furniture) to services (e.g., crowdsourcing, credit cards, and cellphone plans). A national survey has suggested that almost half of consumers try to negotiate deals with salespeople on everyday offerings. Among consumers who choose to negotiate, 89% of them succeed at least once (Consumer Reports 2013). Consumers can save considerably from price negotiations. For example, in the furniture market, successful hagglers can cut $100 to $500 off the retail price (White 2012). In the automobile market, bargaining room for a new car consists of approximately 10%–20% of the dealer’s profit margin (Federal Trade Commission 2019). When price is negotiable, consumers read online reviews not only to evaluate the quality and fit of a product to their needs (Chen and Xie 2008,Kwark et al. 2014) but also to assess their bargaining power to negotiate a lower price. According toConsumer Reports (2013), one common strategy used by successful hagglers is to check online reviews to see how much others paid. To negotiate a smart deal, consumers look into different elements of online reviews. For example, the historical price reported by reviewers can be used as a reference point for potential shoppers to decide what price to accept. Ratings inform consumers of the pros and cons of a product compared with its competitors. Textual comments frequently contain haggling experiences reviewed by recent buyers. From reading such reviews, consumers learn useful tactics in every phase of the negotiation process. Therefore, it is crucial to understand the multiple impacts of online reviews on consumer choice and how they affect profits in this context. It can help sellers craft effective marketing strategies and help platforms discover valuable review content to monetize. The objective of this paper is to understand how reviews affect product choices when price is negotiable. In this context, online reviews may affect consumer choice in two ways. One is by affecting the baseline preference, and the other is by affecting the bargaining power. Although the literature has mainly examined the effect of reviews through the baseline preference, the effect through negotiated price and bargaining power has been largely ignored. We collaborated with a leading Chinese automobile platform and obtained data on product choice and online reviews from January 2014 to December 2015. We identified four review characteristics that might affect a consumer’s product choice and purchase price:average rating,volume,historical price, anddiscount frequency. Our reduced-form regression results suggest that online reviews affect product choices and the negotiated price. We then incorporated online reviews into a Nash bargaining framework to explain a consumer’s negotiated price and product choice (Nash 1950,Chen et al. 2008). In our structural model, online reviews enter a consumer’s utility function in two ways: by affecting a consumer’s baseline preference for a product and by affecting the bargaining power. We estimated our model using a simulated maximum likelihood approach. Our analysis confirms our hypothesis that reviews may affect product choices in multiple ways such that they affect not only a consumer’s baseline preference but also the bargaining power. We find thataverage ratingandhistorical priceare negatively associated with consumer bargaining power, whilediscount frequencyis positively associated with it. We supplemented the empirical analyses with four laboratory experiments to test the causal links of the effects. We conducted two counterfactual analyses based on our sample. In the first experiment, we study how a change in review characteristics affects seller profits. We find that average rating has the largest impact on profits among all four characteristics: Compared with a product with an average rating of 3.72, the dealer profit can be increased by 93.75% for a product with an average rating of 4.60. An increase by one standard deviation in volume can boost profits from 3.72 million CNY1to 5.12 million CNY, which is a37.63%increase. In the second experiment, we decomposed and quantified the importance of the bargaining power mechanism versus the baseline preference mechanism. Our counterfactual analysis suggests that the bargaining power mechanism is the dominant mechanism for review features such as historical price (accounting for55.43%change in profits) and discount frequency (accounting for almost all change in profits). Ignoring the bargaining power mechanism can lead to a biased estimation of the effect of review characteristics on seller profits. Our paper offers several theoretical and managerial contributions. Our theoretical contributions are twofold. First, we provide a novel framework to decompose the multiple mechanisms online reviews have on demand in the negotiation context. When the purchase price is negotiated, online reviews affect demand not only through the baseline preference but also through the negotiated price. This bargaining power mechanism, despite its importance, has been largely overlooked by previous literature, and our paper aims to fill this knowledge gap. Second, we develop an empirical strategy to identify the bargaining power and separate the bargaining power mechanism from the baseline preference mechanism. We leverage the marginal cost information for identification, as it affects the negotiated price, but should not affect consumer baseline preference. We also offer several managerial implications and recommendations to sellers and review platforms. First, our findings can help sellers optimize their reputation management and selling strategies. For example, our analysis uncovers a potential negative impact of offering consumers discounts in exchange for positive reviews, a popular selling practice. If the buyer posts any price and discount information in the review, future consumers are likely to leverage this information to negotiate a low purchase price, thereby hurting seller profits. Our counterfactual analysis suggests that if the historical price posted in online reviews lowers by one standard deviation, seller profits can drop by22.90%. Similarly, a one-standard-deviation increase in the discount frequency information can bring down seller profits by16.47%. In addition, we find that salespeople can leverage the high rating of a product to convince a consumer to pay a higher price for a product and achieve a higher profit margin, as10.19%of the change in profits comes from the bargaining power mechanism. Second, our research offers insights to review platforms for content design and monetization opportunities. As review features such as historical price and discount frequency offer consumers great economic value through price negotiation, a review platform may consider charging consumer fees for accessing certain review information. We organize the rest of this study as follows. We first review the related literature and then discuss the empirical context and descriptive evidence.Section 4proposes a model to capture these mechanisms and presents the identification strategy. We report the estimation and counterfactual results inSection 5. InSection 6, we describe the experimental study. Finally, we conclude inSection 7.
article.2. Literature Review: Our paper is related to two streams of literature: online reviews and negotiation. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to demonstrate the multiple impacts of reviews on consumer product choices under negotiated price. It has been well established in economics, marketing, and information systems that online reviews are of great value to modern shoppers. One widely examined mechanism is that online reviews may affect demand through preference (Archak et al. 2011,Wu et al. 2015,Huang et al. 2019). Although these papers have covered a wide range of products and services, such as books, movies, video games, hotels, and restaurants (Zhu and Zhang 2010,Ghose et al. 2012,Lee et al. 2015), none have considered a buying situation that involves price negotiation. When the price is negotiable, online reviews may affect bargaining power and subsequently, the negotiated price. Only a handful of papers have looked into how reviews affect price. For example,Lucking-Reiley et al. (2007)find an association between an auction price and a seller’s feedback rating for collectible one-cent coins on eBay;Li and Hitt (2010)propose an analytical model to illustrate how online reviews influence a firm’s optimal pricing strategy;Ghose et al. (2007)find the price premium charged by sellers in an online used-good market is affected by user feedback;Pavlou and Dimoka (2006)suggest that the benevolence of text comments has great explanatory power over the variation of price premiums in eBay’s online auction marketplace; andLiu et al. (2017)build a two-period duopoly model to illustrate how online reviews affect firms’ pricing strategies. A common finding is that when price is not fixed, positive reviews are likely to be associated with a higher price. This finding applies to different price settings; that is, price is either set by the seller or determined by an online auction. None of these papers consider price negotiation, which is an important context in durable goods markets. Our paper complements this stream of literature by studying how different review characteristics help consumers achieve a more advantageous position in bargaining with the seller. We combined both the numerical measures with a textual mining approach to explore multiple aspects of review information. Previous studies have found that important numerical features such as valence and volume may affect a consumer’s product choice (Chevalier and Mayzlin 2006,Liu 2006). Other papers have emphasized the textual content of user reviews as an important factor (Archak et al. 2011,Ghose et al. 2012,Netzer et al. 2012). In this paper, we combined these two approaches to comprehensively understand the differential effect of review features on product choice and negotiated price. We find that historical price and discount frequency information provided in reviews can greatly help consumers negotiate a lower price. Our work also closely draws on the bargaining literature. Ever sinceNash (1950)develops the pioneering bargaining framework, it has been used to characterize the price negotiation in various contexts such as the labor market, the daily deal market, the medical device market, and the automobile market, among others (Ellis and Fender 1985,Grennan 2013,Zhang and Chung 2020,Zhang et al. 2021). A prior example closely related to our work isChen et al. (2008)in which they develop a structural model to empirically study consumer product choice and price negotiation behaviors in the automobile market. Although both studies investigate price negotiation in the automobile market and adopt the Nash bargaining framework, our paper is fundamentally different fromChen et al. (2008)in terms of the model and the results. Specifically, our modeling framework takes into account the impact of online reviews on bargaining outcomes, which is a critical factor that Chen et al. do not consider. The proposed model allows us to decompose and quantify the importance of the baseline preference mechanism and the bargaining power mechanism for online reviews to affect seller profits. Moreover, we demonstrate the importance of leveraging online reviews to help sellers design better pricing strategies and review platforms improve content management practices. In addition, this paper advances our understanding of the determinants of bargaining power. Although bargaining power is an important construct, few studies have explored contributing factors. As stated inBinmore et al. (1986, p. 186), bargaining power captures “the asymmetry in the two parties’ beliefs about the negotiation environment.” If we follow this definition, the information processed by both parties should be an important factor. This hypothesis has been supported by past studies. For example,Ratchford and Srinivasan (1993)find that a more knowledgeable consumer is more likely to get a favorable bargaining outcome in the automobile market. In a similar spirit,Zettelmeyer et al. (2006)argue that it might be easier for consumers with Internet access to negotiate a lower price. In our context, the information source is user-generated content. We bridge the knowledge gap by asking if online reviews affect consumer bargaining power, and if so, by how much. We find that several key review features, that is,, historical price and discount frequency, might have such an impact on shaping automobile shoppers’ beliefs about relative bargaining positions with dealers.
article.3. Empirical Context: We first discuss our empirical context. We focused on consumers’ car choices for two reasons. First, it is common for consumers and dealers to negotiate a purchase price in this market (D’Haultfoeuille et al. 2019,Huang 2020). The final transaction price is greatly determined by both the dealer’s and the consumer’s bargaining power (Ratchford and Srinivasan 1993). Second, online reviews are an important source of information consumers use to evaluate a product, to learn bargaining tactics, and to seek out the best deal (J.D. Power 2015). According to a consumer survey of the Chinese automobile market conducted by the consulting firm Capgemini, 80% of respondents reported that their car purchase decision had been affected by social media in particular from online consumer review forums (Capgemini 2015). In particular, 66% of car buyers visited automobile review platform to assist their purchase decision (J.D. Power 2015). 3.1. Data We obtained data from a leading online automobile platform in China. This platform provides a major online source of automobile information, such as vehicle prices, dealership locations, and inventory listings on all aspects of car purchases and ownership. It is not only the go-to place for consumers to search for automobile information but also one of the largest online forums for car-owners to share their shopping and driving experiences. With approximately 20 million active users, the platform’s user review section is one of the most popular among potential shoppers. To ensure reviews are coming from actual buyers, the platform has taken stringent measures, requiring reviewers to provide critical proofs of ownership. For example, for a review to be approved by the platform, reviewers must submit photos of the invoice, receipt, or car key as proof and to post photos of themselves with their cars. Reviewers are also asked to provide a detailed description up to the trim level of their purchased products. Posts will be deleted if reviewers fail to complete these requirements. We collected a comprehensive data set of consumer reviews from the platform from January 2012, when the first review was posted, to December 2015. For each review, the consumer reveals detailed purchase-related information, including the purchased vehicle, when and where the purchase was made, and transaction price. The platform also asks reviewers to rate the purchased product on a one- to five-star scale along with eight different attributes: comfort, horsepower, exterior design, interior styling, fuel consumption, handling, space, and price–performance ratio. Reviewers also write textual comments to justify their numerical ratings. In addition, we observe the post date of each review. We used this data set to construct consumers’ product choices and review characteristics. 3.1.1. Product Choice and Transaction Price. Because the platform has checked the authenticity of these reviews, we treated each reviewer as a consumer and each review as a purchase record. Our conversation with the platform manager suggested that consumers who post reviews should be largely representative of the consumer base of the Chinese automobile market as the platform has a large user base. Prior studies have used similar methods to infer a consumer’s product purchase decision. For example,Huang (2019)uses information included in personal photographs from an online community to infer a consumer’s camera replacement history. Each purchase record contained the product-related information, the date and location of the purchase, and a transaction price. We constructed consumers’ product choices and transaction prices using reviews in which consumers reported a new car purchase during January 2014 to December 2015. We chose this time period to match our data on dealers’ marginal cost, which provided an important source of information for identification, and we will discuss it later in detail. We considered new car purchases as they constituted 80% of the Chinese automobile market (Capgemini 2015) and dropped observations of any used car purchases. We find that the reported transaction prices vary considerably across consumers for the same model and trim. We focused on compact-level sedans, as this category is the most popular among consumers. We defined a product as a unique combination of year, make, and model and focused on 16 of the most popular products in this category, which consisted of 64.95% of the market share during our sample period. For the rest of the products in this category with less than3%market share, we aggregated them as one and defined it asother products. The review characteristics and the product attributes for other products are the average of the remaining products. To ensure that our analysis was representative of the Chinese automobile market, we matched the choice data with the actual market share based on the product name and year of manufacture. We had a total of 78,896 reviews from 2014 to 2015 from which we randomly drew 30,000 observations among the 16 products with the product’s actual market share as the sampling weight. We then randomly drew 16,189 observations from the choice set of the other products to match with its market share of 35.05% during this period. The final choice data contained 46,189 observations. 3.1.2. Review Characteristics. We constructed several variables measuring different review characteristics. First, we includedaverage ratingandvolume, as prior studies have shown that these two are important to consumers’ product choices (Chevalier and Mayzlin 2006,Liu 2006). The platform provides the aggregated average rating and volume for each product and updates this information on a regular basis. We computed theaverage ratingas a cumulative average of the numerical ratings andvolumeas the cumulative number of reviews. In addition, we consideredhistorical pricebecause hagglers were likely to check the prices paid by others as a reference point. We measuredhistorical priceusing the cumulative average of the reviewer-reported price. Also, reviewers occasionally post detailed negotiation process and discount-related information in their comments. We postulated that these textual comments allowed future shoppers to evaluate how much bargaining room they had with the seller. We therefore constructed the variablediscount frequencyto measure the frequency that textual comments contained discount-related information. We adopted a text-mining approach: for each review, if the consumer mentioned the negotiation process or the discount offered by the dealer, we set the dummy to be one and zero otherwise. We find that 6.7% textual comments include the word “discount,” which indicates it is common for reviewers to share negotiation experiences. We present a few examples of the textual comments that include discount-related information inTable 1. In these reviews, consumers go into great detail regarding their price negotiation–from the best negotiation time to which color is harder to bargain for a low price. The richness in review content justifies our assumption that the incentives for consumers to read reviews are not limited to product quality evaluation, but also to acquire information related to price and negotiation. The definition of all four review characteristics is summarized inTable 2. We present a time series plot of all four review characteristics for a representative product inFigure 1. Table 1.Textual Review Content on Price Negotiation and Bargaining Discount Table 1.Textual Review Content on Price Negotiation and Bargaining Discount Notes.This table offers three examples of textual consumer reviews that are related to price negotiation and bargaining discounts. We chose three representative reviewers and three representative products. Table 2.Variable Definition for Review Characteristics Table 2.Variable Definition for Review Characteristics 3.1.3. Other Control Variables. To control for other variables that may also predict choice and price, we collected data on product-specific information and dealer-specific information. By scraping the information of product attributes, we collected data on several important variables, including the manufacturer’s suggested retail price (MSRP), country-of-origin, size, max horsepower, fuel consumption,2and so forth. On the same platform, we also observed dealer-side information, such as the list price for a product, their shop addresses, phone numbers, and so forth. In addition, we had data on dealers’ marginal cost to acquire a car from the manufacturer for each product and a monthly market share from January 2014 to December 2015. We matched them with our choice sample based on the product identifiers. 3.1.4. Summary Statistics. We next discuss the descriptive statistics reported inTable 3. The 16 products we included in our sample come from four countries: Germany, the United States, Japan, and Korea. These products are relatively homogeneous in terms of product attributes such as size, max horsepower, and fuel consumption, with the following price range: product 14 has the highest MSRP of 239.90 thousand CNY, whereas product 10 has the lowest MSRP of 74.26 thousand CNY. The report fromTruecar.comsuggests that the average MSRP of the Chinese automobile market is 131 thousand CNY in 2014 (True Car 2018). This price is comparable to the average MSRP of 133 thousand CNY in summary of statistics in our sample. We measured the competition intensity in selling the focal product in an area using the variablenumber of dealers. This variable counts the number of dealers that sell the same product in the same city. We constructed a variabledealer sizeto measure the size of a local dealership. The dummy takes the value of one if the dealer carries equal or more varieties of cars than the national average and zero otherwise. Table 3.Summary of Statistics Table 3.Summary of Statistics Note.Price, MSRP, list price, marginal cost, and historical price are measured in 1,000 CNY. We observed a few interesting patterns in our data. We find that the transaction price is systematically lower than the dealer’s list price, and the list price is systematically lower than the car’s MSRP. In particular, 99.04% of the purchase price lies strictly below the list price. The difference between the transaction price and the list price justifies the anecdotal evidence that consumers routinely negotiate prices with dealers in the automobile market. On average, consumers receive a 9.5% discount on top of the MSRP, and the discounts vary substantially across consumers. 3.1.5. Online Reviews and Negotiated Price. We conducted the following reduced-form regression analysis to test whether the negotiated price is affected by review characteristics. Leti∈I≡{1,…,I}denote consumers andj∈J≡{1,…,J}denote products. We usej=Jto denote that a consumer chooses other products (i.e., a compact-size sedan other than the 16 major ones). The model specification is as follows: We present our regression results inTable 4. We considered different model specifications. In the first specification, we included the product attributes, such asMSRP,size,fuel consumption,country-of-origin, andmax horsepower. In the second one, we dropped the variables of product attributes and controlled for product fixed effects instead. Last, we controlled for year fixed effects and month fixed effects in addition to the second specification. The results were robust in terms of signs and statistical significance. We find thataverage ratingandhistorical pricehave a positive impact on the negotiated price (coefficient = 0.368;p< 0.01 and coefficient = 0.011;p< 0.01), whereasdiscount frequencyis negatively correlated with the negotiated price (coefficient = −0.391;p< 0.01).Volumeis negatively associated with the negotiated price (coefficient = −0.056;p< 0.01). The regression results suggest that review characteristics are strongly correlated with the negotiated price, indicating that online reviews may affect product choices indirectly through the negotiated price. However, we are not sure whether reviews affect price through bargaining power or willingness to pay. Table 4.Evidence of the Impact of Online Reviews on Negotiated Price Table 4.Evidence of the Impact of Online Reviews on Negotiated Price Notes.OLS regression results of the negotiated price on online reviews. Standard errors are in parentheses. This sample includes observations with a transaction price available. We report the results in column 2. 3.1.6. Online Reviews and Product Choice. We next provide empirical evidence of the effects of review characteristics on a consumer’s product choice. We conducted the following multinomial logistic (MNL) regression as stated inEquation (2): Table 5.Evidence of the Impact of Online Reviews on Product Choice Table 5.Evidence of the Impact of Online Reviews on Product Choice Notes.OLS regression results of product choice probability on online reviews. Standard errors are in parentheses. We report the parameter of estimates based on results in column 2 of this table.
article.4. Model and Identification: These regression results provide suggestive evidence of the direct and indirect effect of review characteristics on product choices. Although we know that review characteristics may affect the negotiated price and therefore indirectly affect product choices, we do not know if the effect comes from bargaining power or from a consumer’s willingness to pay for a product. Therefore, we developed a structural model to separate these two effects. 4.1. Proposed Model We proposed a model to reveal the multiple roles that online reviews had on a consumer’s product choice, and we illustrated our conceptual framework inFigure 2. This framework draws upon Nash bargaining model, in which the negotiated price is jointly determined by the willingness to pay (WTP) and the bargaining power. We therefore decomposed the impact of online reviews into two mechanisms: the baseline preference mechanism and the bargaining power mechanism. In the former, online reviews affect consumer’s baseline preference for a product. The change in baseline preference affects choice probability directly and indirectly through the willingness to pay and the negotiated price. In the bargaining power mechanism, reviews affect a consumer’s bargaining power. It will further affect the negotiated price and choice probability. The bargaining power mechanism, despite its importance, has not been studied in the literature of consumer reviews. It is impossible to disentangle these mechanisms and accurately quantify the impact of reviews on the negotiated price using reduced-form regressions. We next discuss our proposed model in detail. Although we choose the automobile market as the context for consistency with our empirical analysis, our model can be easily extended to other durable goods settings, such as home furniture, electronic appliances, or housing. We adopted the framework proposed byChen et al. (2008). In particular, we assume that a consumer makes a simultaneous decision of price negotiation and product choice.3The consumer reads online reviews to understand the baseline preference for a product and to achieve a more favorable bargaining outcome. Mathematically, leti∈I≡{1,…,I}denote consumers andj∈J≡{1,…,J}denote products. A utility-maximization consumerichooses one product from the set ofJproducts. We assume that the consideration set consists of the 16 most popular compact-size sedans plus a composite product which consists of the rest of compact-size sedans with less than 3% market shares. We label the composite good as “other products” and usej=Jto denote it. The utility function forj∈{1,…,J−1}is given by The baseline preferenceδijis determined by the following equation: If productjis purchased by consumeri, we assume that the observed transaction price is an outcome of negotiation plus an error term. Mathematically, the observed price of consumerifor productj,pij4is given by We use the classic Nash bargaining framework (Nash 1950) to model the negotiated price. The equilibrium is characterized as a split in payoffs between a dealer and a consumer. To accommodate the disparities in parties’ beliefs about the market, we allow an asymmetry, namely the bargaining power in splitting profits between the local dealership and the consumer. Mathematically, if consumeripurchases productj, the equilibrium negotiated pricep˜ijsolves the Nash bargaining problem: Hereλij∈(0,1)is a scalar capturing the relative bargaining power of consumeriwith productjat the corresponding dealer. Previous studies have argued that this factor captures parties’ beliefs about the relative bargaining position. We postulate that online reviews, as a valuable information source to modern shoppers, may affect such a belief. We will further illustrate the review characteristics that may affect this belief. Solving the maximization problem, we obtain the asymmetric Nash bargaining solution (Binmore et al. 1986) of equilibrium price-negotiation outcome as a function betweenwijandcj: The negotiated price in equilibrium consists of three parts: (1) the consumer’s willingness to paywij, (2) the dealer’s marginal costcj, and (3) the relative bargaining powerλij. Among these three parts, online reviews may affect bothwijandλij. The joint impact on negotiated price is therefore complex and can only be identified by estimating a structural model. For example, because consumers naturally prefer a higher-rated product,wijshould be higher for a higher-rated car than for a lower-rated one. Conversely, dealers are likely to leverage this high rating to achieve a more favorable position in a price negotiation. The relative bargaining power for consumerλijshould therefore be lower. Mathematically, the relative bargaining power of consumerifor productj,λij, is given by We impose a logit function form on the bargaining power to ensure thatλijlies in the range between zero and one, that is,0<λij<1. We propose that the relative bargaining power can be explained by two parts: the review featureRj,t(i)−1and the local market-specific variablesMij, such as the number of dealers and the dealer size.γs are the corresponding parameters of interest that we want to estimate. We now discuss how we model the consumer’s willingness to paywij. To capture the stylized fact that a rational consumer is never willing to pay more than the list pricepijL, we impose the upper bound on a consumer’s willingness to pay to bepijL. This assumption is consistent with our empirical observation, as 99.04% of the sampled consumers have a purchase price strictly below the list price. Specifically, we assume a consumer’s maximum willingness to paywijis given by We define a consumers’ reservation valuerijas the price that makes the consumer indifferent between acquiring the product and the next best alternative. That is, We can then rewriteEquation (8)as If we plugEquation (5)intoEquation (12), we have We can then rewriteEquation (3)andEquation (4)as 4.2. Identification Previously, we illustrate the effect of multiple mechanisms that reviews have on product choices. Reviews may not only directly affect product choice by influencing the baseline preference, they can also indirectly impact product choices by affecting the consumer’s willingness to pay for a product, as well as the relative bargaining power. These two effects translate into the negotiated price and therefore affect product choices indirectly. As the objective of this study is to understand the different mechanisms online reviews have on product choices, a clean identification strategy is essential. In this section, we discuss our theoretical and empirical identification strategy. Theoretically, we show how the source of variation in our data allows us to identify our parameters of interest; empirically, we explain how we can separate these mechanisms with the dealers’ marginal cost information. 4.2.1. Theoretical Identification. Letθ≡(θB,θP)≡(α,β,γ,ση)denote the set of parameters of interest:θP≡(α,β)stands for preference related parameters, andθB≡(γ,ση)denotes bargaining power related parameters. We separateθBfromθP, as we identify them from two different sources of variations: (1) the transaction price and (2) the individual product choice. We first illustrate intuitively how these two variables allow us to identify each parameter. We identify the bargaining power parameters using the covariation between the negotiated price and the covariates. The correlation between the transaction price and online reviews allows us to identifyγ, which is the bargaining power coefficient. For example, suppose that when thehistorical priceis higher, we observe a higher transaction price on average, holding other factors constant. We then know that the bargaining power coefficientγhistoricalpriceis negative. Following the same logic, we can identifyγM. The difference between the model-predicted price and the observed transaction price allows us to identifyση. Suppose there exists a fraction of consumers whose reservation value for a product is greater than the list price. Their maximum willingness to pay is by definition the product’s list price. Under this condition,Equation (13)can be written as If we observe both the transaction price and the dealer’s marginal cost, we can then identify the bargaining powerλijas a function of covariates, thereby identifyingγs. The marginal utility of incomeαand consumer preferenceβare identified from a consumer’s product choice probability. We can identify the marginal utility of incomeαfrom the change in the likelihood of choosing this product associated with a change in the negotiated price.βRs are identified in a similar way. If the rating of a product increases, for example, and consumers are more likely to purchase the product, then the coefficient for rating should be positive. By imposing functional form assumptions on the distributions of unobserved heterogeneityϵijand the price errorηij, we can then fully identify our parameters of interest. This theoretical identification strategy follows that ofJindal and Newberry (2018).6 4.2.2. Empirical Identification. We now shift our focus to the empirical identification of different impacts of online reviews. For any consumer withrij<pijL, we can rewriteEquation (13)as Consider a simple case in which That is, a consumer’s baseline preference for a productδijand the relative bargaining powerλijare solely decided by online reviews.7For the sake of simplicity, we assume the consumer’s second best option is to choose the other productsj=Jand the utility isϵiJ. We then know fromEquation (12)that If we plugEquation (19)andEquation (20)intoEquation (17), we have Suppose we letγ=0, that is, we shut down the bargaining power effect; thenEquation (21)becomes This equation indicates that even if online reviews do not affect the bargaining power, they may affect the negotiated price via the willingness to pay. This suggests that we cannot separately identifyβRandγRby simply regressing the negotiated price on online reviews. Although previously we have found evidence thatRj,t(i)−1is related topij, we cannot conclude thatRj,t(i)−1affects bargaining power without further evidence. 4.2.3. Identification Using the Marginal Cost Information. We will first show that we can separately identify these two routes by which reviews affect product choices if we observe the dealers’ marginal costs. If the marginal increase in negotiated price caused by an increase in the marginal cost does not vary with respect to online reviews, it indicates that the bargaining power effect does not exist. We illustrate the intuition for our identification strategy inFigure 3. Suppose we observe the negotiated price in the following four cases: good reviews, bad reviews, high marginal cost, and low marginal cost. If online reviews affect both the baseline preference and the bargaining power, the marginal increase in the negotiated price from low marginal cost to high marginal cost should be lower for good reviews than for bad reviews. This corresponds to the case inFigure 3(a). If online reviews only affect the baseline preference, the marginal increase in the negotiated price from low marginal cost to high marginal cost should be the same for a product with good reviews or with bad reviews. This corresponds to the case inFigure 3(b). Mathematically, if we take the first-order partial derivative of negotiated price on the marginal cost inEquation (21), we have These two partial derivatives are different depending on whether online reviews affect bargaining power or not. When they affect bargaining power,∂p˜ij/∂cjhas a logit form overRj,t(i)−1. When they do not affect it,∂p˜ij/∂cjis constant. 4.2.4. Empirical Evidence of the Link Between Reviews and Bargaining Power. We now provide some suggestive evidence for the existence of the bargaining power effect. The mathematical derivation suggests that the existence of the bargaining power effect can be shown by regressing the negotiated price on the interaction terms between the marginal cost and online reviews. Our reduced-form regression model has a specification as follows:8 We present the regression results of negotiated price on covariates inTable 6. We find that the interaction terms of marginal cost and review characteristics have statistically significant effects on the negotiated price, indicating that online reviews may also affect a consumer’s bargaining power. Table 6.Empirical Evidence of Link Between Reviews and Bargaining Power Table 6.Empirical Evidence of Link Between Reviews and Bargaining Power Notes.OLS regression results of negotiated price on covariates. Standard errors are in parentheses.
article.5. Empirical Analysis: In this section, we first discuss how we construct the likelihood for estimation and then present the parameter estimates and the counterfactual analysis. 5.1. Estimation We estimated our model using a simulated maximum likelihood approach. Letyijdenote consumeri’s purchase decision for productj. For each of the productsj∈{1,…,J}, the outcome variables consist of two parts: (1) the product purchase decisionyijand (2) the actual transaction pricepij. Recall that in our model, we included error terms in the predicted price and utility functions to accommodate unobserved factors that might affect consumer behaviors and made parametric assumptions on their distributions accordingly. We first derive the probability of an outcome conditional on knowing these unobservables and then integrate it over their distributions to obtain the likelihood function for estimation. Specifically, conditional on knowing the unobserved consumer characteristicsϵijand the error termηij, the probability that a researcher observes consumeribuying productjwith a negotiated pricepijis given by We account for the fact that researchers do not observeϵijandηijand solve this problem by integrating the probability over the distribution ofϵijandηij. Recall that we assume thatϵijfollows an i.i.d. type I extreme value distribution with mean equal to zero and a variance equal toπ/6,ηijfollows an i.i.d. distribution withN(0,ση2). After integration, the unconditional probability of a researcher observing consumerichoosing the productjwith a purchase pricepijis given by The log-likelihood of observing a series of outcomes by consumeri∈I≡{1,2,…,I}from productj∈J≡{1,2,…,J}can be written as One challenge we face is that as the idiosyncratic error terms enter the consumer willingness to pay as we illustrate inEquation (12), the integral inEquation (27)does not have a closed-form solution even with the logit distribution. To overcome this challenge, we simulated the demand-side shockϵand computed the choice probability based on the simulated outcomes. This approach helped us to obtain a numerical integral for our unconditional likelihood. Specifically, the simulated log-likelihood is derived as follows. Let a dummy variableyijsdenote the product choice a consumer makes in simulation draws;yijsequals one if modeljmaximizes the utility and zero otherwise. The simulated log-likelihood function can be written as The simulated log-likelihood is not smooth because of the discreteness of the dummy variableyijs. We therefore cannot use a gradient-search algorithm to search for the maximum value for our log-likelihood function. A remedy to this problem is to take numerous simulation draws, but to do so is computationally costly. As illustrated inHonka (2014), an alternative method to solve this problem is to smooth the dummy variable using a kernel-smoothed frequency simulator (McFadden 1989). We smoothed the dummy variables with a multivariate-scaled logistic cumulative distribution function (CDF) as inGumbel (1961): Replacing dummyyijswith a kernel-smoothed frequency simulatorF(ω;m), our log-likelihood function becomes 5.2. Parameter Estimates We next present the estimation results from our structural model. We first show how our proposed model outperforms the benchmark one in terms of in-sample fit, and then present the estimates and results of robustness checks. Last, we discuss the counterfactual analysis. InTable 7, we compare the model fit of three specifications. Model 1 does not include review information. In model 2, reviews only appear in consumer baseline preference. Model 3 is the proposed one. We find that our proposed model outperforms the two benchmark models in both the Akaike information criterion (AIC) and the Bayesian information criterion (BIC). Table 7.Model Fit Comparison Table 7.Model Fit Comparison Notes.The AIC and BIC used for comparison are computed based on the specification of column 1 inTable 8. We report our parameter estimates inTable 8. Our results lend support to the two mechanisms. Consistent with previous studies (Chevalier and Mayzlin 2006,Wu et al. 2015), we find thataverage ratingpositively affects a consumer’s baseline preference: a more positive rating is associated with a higher baseline preference (coefficient = 1.604,p< 0.01). We find a positive effect forvolume(coefficient = 0.508,p< 0.01). This finding is consistent withLiu (2006). We find that the coefficient forhistorical priceis positive and statistically significant (coefficient = 0.508,p< 0.01), whereas the coefficient fordiscount frequencyis significantly negative (coefficient = −0.007,p< 0.01). As previous studies argue that price signals the quality of a product (Erdem et al. 2008), we believe that review information such as historical price and discount frequency may affect a consumer’s baseline preference for a product in a similar way. A higher historical price should increase a consumer’s quality perception for a product, whereas more frequent discount should have a negative impact. Table 8.Model Estimates Table 8.Model Estimates Notes.Structural estimation results on the parameters of interest. Standard errors are in parentheses. We report the estimates based on results in column 3 of this table. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. We next report findings on other covariates. We present the estimation results inTable 9. Our results are consistent with findings in the economic and marketing literature (Berry et al. 1995). We find that consumers on average prefer cars with better fuel consumption and higher horsepower. We find a significant effect of the variable country-of-origin: consumers on average prefer Japanese cars over American cars, German cars, or Korean cars. Our estimates also suggest that consumers who have morenumber of dealersavailable in an area have a higher preference for a product (coefficient = 0.039,p< 0.01). We also find that consumers prefer a large dealership (coefficient = 0.091,p< 0.01). Table 9.Product Attributes Estimates Table 9.Product Attributes Estimates Notes.Structural estimation results on parameters of interest. Standard errors are in parentheses. Fuel consumption is measured as the fuel consumed per hundred kilometers. The variable country-of-origin can take four levels: Germany, Japan, United States, or Korea. The dummy variable country-of-origin: Korea is normalized to be the base level in our estimation. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. More importantly, our estimates suggest a strong correlation between review features and a consumer’s relative bargaining power. In particular, we find consumers will have a lower bargaining power for a product with a higher average rating (coefficient = −0.375,p< 0.01), allowing the dealer to be in a more advantageous position in negotiation. Similarly, the coefficient forvolumeis negative (coefficient = −0.256,p< 0.01). We also find thathistorical priceis negatively associated with a consumer’s relative bargaining power (coefficient = −0.484,p< 0.01). Online reviews containing dealer-discount information may give consumers more bargaining power (coefficient = 0.764,p< 0.01). Other than online reviews, we find consumers enjoy a larger bargaining power in a more competitive market (coefficient = 0.169,p< 0.01). What is worth noticing is that as sampled consumers come from a population who are more engaged with online review platforms, the readers should be cautious when applying results to the entire population of the Chinese automobile market. The estimated effect can be stronger. Our analysis reveals the estimated bargaining power varies across consumers, as illustrated byFigure 4. The average consumer bargaining power of this market is 0.484, indicating a reasonably balanced position for both parties. Dealers are in a slightly advantageous position when bargaining with consumers in our context. We also find strong heterogeneity in bargaining powers across products and present the histogram inFigure 5. Consumers have the highest bargaining power with product 16 and lowest one with product 14. 5.3. Robustness Check We estimated our model with a few different settings as robustness checks. We considered cases in which we added product attributes, product fixed effects, product-year fixed effects, and product-month fixed effects. We present the results in columns 1 to 3 ofTable 8. The key findings remain largely the same. 5.3.1. Endogeneity Correction. We were aware of the potential endogeneity in our proposed model and adopted the instrumental variable approach to mitigate this problem. In particular, we considered the potential endogeneity on two variables: list price and number of dealers. We used the inventory availability as the instrumental variable for the list price. We measured inventory availability by the number of cars available for sale in a dealership. We believe this is a valid instrument because inventory availability affects a dealer’s decision to set list price but should not affect demand directly as it is not observable by consumers. We usednumber of nearby dealersas the instrumental variable fornumber of dealersfollowingNevo (2001)andHausman et al. (1994). We definednumber of nearby dealersas the average number of dealers selling the same product in nearby cities. The Instrumental Variable and the potentially endogenous variable should be significantly correlated due to supply-side factors such as the manufacturer’s strategic location decisions and cost minimization strategies. However, the number of nearby city dealerships may not drive the demand of the focal city. According to Chinese regulation, the owner needs to return to the place of purchase for any quality-related problems. For convenience, consumers usually prefer to purchase from local dealers. We adopted the control function approach to correct for endogeneity in nonlinear models (e.g., choice model) as inPetrin and Train (2010). We first regressed the endogenous variable on the instrumental variable and other covariates. We then estimated our structural model with the predicted residual in the first stage as an additional variable. The intuition is that the first-stage regression allows us to separate the exogenous variation from the residuals. By including the residuals as an additional explanatory variable, we control for the endogenous part of the error term, and the remaining part of the error term should not be correlated with the endogenous variable. We can thus achieve an unbiased estimation of the effect of the focal variable. We present the estimation results inTable 10. We find that the results are consistent in terms of signs and significance levels. Table 10.Model Estimates with Endogeneity Correction Table 10.Model Estimates with Endogeneity Correction Notes.Structural estimation results on the parameters of interest. Standard errors are in parentheses. We report the estimates based on results in column 3 of this table. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. 5.3.2. Unobserved Consumer and Dealer Heterogeneity. We relaxed the assumption that consumers have homogenous tastes and adopted the random-coefficient model as inBerry et al. (1995)andJindal and Newberry (2018)to account for the unobserved heterogeneity in consumer preferences and bargaining powers. Because we only observe one purchase record for each customer, we assume that consumers from the same location have common preference. Furthermore, as we model one representative dealer for a product in each location, unobserved heterogeneity across dealers is also incorporated through the random-coefficient specification across locations. Table 11reports the estimation results. Allowing unobserved heterogeneity in tastes improves model fit but does not qualitatively change the results. We find heterogeneity in consumer baseline preferences but not so much in their bargaining power. Results remain qualitatively the same for the means of the distributions. Table 11.Model Estimates with Heterogeneous Preference Table 11.Model Estimates with Heterogeneous Preference Notes.Structural estimation results on the parameters of interest. Standard errors are in parentheses. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. 5.4. Counterfactual Analyses Thus far, we provided evidence of the two mechanisms at operation that explain how online reviews affect consumer product choices. One advantage of our structural model is to perform counterfactual analyses. To optimize their online word-of-mouth (WOM) strategy and enhance their negotiation outcome, sellers must not only understand how changes in review characteristics can affect their profits but also distinguish if the effect comes from bargaining power. In the counterfactual analyses, we first examine how profits change with respect to a change in review characteristics. We then decompose the bargaining power mechanism from the baseline preference mechanism and quantify the importance of each mechanism. 5.4.1. How Review Characteristics Affect Profit Change. In the first counterfactual experiment, we study how a change in review characteristics affects a dealer’s profits. The implementation is as follows. We computed the change in the average dealer profits when we increased the four review characteristics,average rating,volume,historical price,anddiscount frequencyby one standard deviation, respectively. We start by defining the average dealer profitsπ¯SQin the status quo, where all the review characteristics stay unchanged in the following equation: Here,Pr(yij=1)denotes the choice probability for consumerifor productj;p(δ(Rj,t−1),λ(Rj,t−1))indicates the negotiated price when the review characteristics take the value ofRj,t−1; andcjrefers to the marginal cost for the product. For the sake of simplicity, we drop theiin subscriptt(i)and usetto represent the purchase time. We compute the average profits across products to minimize the heterogeneity across products. The profit function in the counterfactual caseπ¯CFcan be defined as Table 12reports the results. The average profits across products are 3.720 million CNY, approximately 531.5 thousand U.S. dollars. We found that compared with a product with an average rating of 3.722, the dealer profit can be increased by 93.75% for a product with an average rating of 4.604.9An increase by one standard deviation in volume can boost profits from 3.720 million CNY to 5.120 million CNY, which is a37.63%increase. A standard deviation increase in historical price is related to22.90%improvement in profits. We find an opposite effect in discount frequency: One-standard-deviation increase in discount frequency can bring down the profits by16.47%. Table 12.The Effect of Review Characteristics on Profits Table 12.The Effect of Review Characteristics on Profits Note.The table reports the change in total profits with respect to one standard deviation increase in each review characteristics. To study the heterogeneous effect of review characteristics on profits by product type, we conducted an additional analysis. We first classified the products based on their countries of origin and then calculated the average change in profits with respect to a change in review characteristics for each product type. Our findings are present inTable 13. The results demonstrate strong heterogeneity for the effect of review characteristics on profits for products with different countries of origin. For example, we find that the effect of discount frequency is most pronounced for the U.S. products (−20.01%), whereas the impact of discount frequency is the lowest for Korean products (−13.69%). Additionally, our analysis indicates that historical price has the most substantial effect on German products (21.61%) compared with Korean products, which are least affected (17.13%).Figure 6demonstrates the heterogeneity in profit change. Table 13.Effect of Review Characteristics on Profits by Product Type Table 13.Effect of Review Characteristics on Profits by Product Type Note.The table reports the change in total profits with respect to one standard deviation increase in each review characteristics by products of different countries of origin. 5.4.2. Importance of the Baseline Preference Mechanism vs. the Bargaining Power Mechanism. To decompose the bargaining power mechanism and the baseline preference mechanism, we provide the second policy simulation. The analysis allows us to separately measure the effect via change in baseline utilities and effect via change in the bargaining power and identify the dominant mechanism. Mathematically, we define the overall effect as Equations (36)and (37) merit discussion. We define the bargaining power effect as the percentage change in profits when we increase the review characteristics that affect the bargaining power by one standard deviation and let the review characteristics that enter the baseline utility stay unchanged. The bargaining power mechanism will affect demand indirectly by affecting the negotiated price. We further define the baseline preference effect as one minus the bargaining power effect. We report the results inTable 14. For average rating,10.19%of change in profits comes from the bargaining power mechanism, whereas the rest of them comes from the traditional baseline preference mechanism. For volume,12.19%of change in profits can be attributed to the bargaining power mechanism. We find that bargaining power mechanism is the dominant mechanism for historical price (account for55.43%change in profits) and discount frequency (account for almost all change in profits). Ignoring the bargaining power mechanism can lead to a biased estimation of the effect of the four review characteristics on seller profits. Table 14.Importance of the Baseline Preference Mechanism vs. the Bargaining Power Mechanism Table 14.Importance of the Baseline Preference Mechanism vs. the Bargaining Power Mechanism Note.The table reports the decomposition of the bargaining power mechanism and the baseline preference mechanism in the change of total profits with respect to one standard deviation increase in each review characteristics.
article.6. Experimental Evidence: Because we have found evidence that reviews may affect a consumer’s baseline preference and relative bargaining power from the secondary data, we further test that these mechanisms are at work using an experimental study. Consistent with the four review features in our secondary data analysis, we conducted four experiments. In each experiment, we focused on average rating, historical price, discount frequency, and volume, respectively. The dependent variables were baseline preference, bargaining power, and willingness to accept the offer, which was the proxy for the final transaction price. 6.1. Participants and Procedure All four experiments adopted a one-factor, two-level, between-subjects design. We recruited around 200 participants from MTurk for each experiment. FollowingYan and Pena-Marin (2017), we informed all participants that the purpose of this study was to understand price negotiation in the new car market. They were instructed to imagine that they were buying a domestic sedan. The MSRP of the car was listed at $29,195. They went to a local dealership to negotiate the best price. After a few rounds of negotiation, the latest offer was $28,319. The participants were asked to indicate whether they were willing to accept this deal or to continue with negotiations (i.e., reject it and make a counter offer). In all four experiments, we kept the MSRP and the dealer’s offer constant and manipulated the review condition. Participants’ preference for the product was captured by the response to the question “How much do you like this car?” (1 = like a great deal, 7 = dislike a great deal). To measure the consumer’s bargaining power, we asked the participants to decide if the seller or the buyer “has more advantages in the price negotiation” and “which party has a higher bargaining power” along a seven-point scale (1 = buyer, 7 = seller). 6.2. Results In the first experiment, the participants (31.16%female,Mage=33.63,SDage=10.54) were randomly assigned to different average rating conditions (high versus low). We showed participants an aggregated consumer review of this product as displayed in the Online Appendix. In the high-rating condition, the average rating was 9/10, whereas in the low-rating condition, the average rating was 4.5/10. We removed the incomplete cases and had a total of 199 observations. Our manipulation on the high- versus low-rating condition was successful, as participants perceived the rating to be higher in the high-rating condition (M=5.69,SD=0.88) than in the low-rating condition (M=3.98,SD=1.72;F(1,197)=83.41,p< 0.01). We averaged the scores of the two questions on the bargaining power to form an index, and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) on this index suggested that the effect was significant (F(1,197)=10.28,p< 0.01). Participants in the high-rating condition perceived sellers to have higher bargaining power (M=4.32,SD=1.45), whereas those in the low-rating condition believed that buyers had a higher bargaining power (M=3.61,SD=1.67). In terms of preference, we found that participants who faced a high-rating condition liked this product more (M=2.49,SD=1.34) than those who faced a low-rating condition (M=3.73,SD=1.76,F(1,197)=31.87,p< 0.01). We ran a logistic regression in which participants’ decisions (to accept or not) were regressed on high average rating. As expected, we found that participants who faced a high-rating condition were more likely to accept this offer (64.86%) compared with those who faced a low-rating condition (46.59%,t= 2.60,p< 0.05). All these results were consistent with our findings on the secondary data. For the second experiment, we randomly assigned the participants (42.70%female,Mage=34.98,SDage=10.01) to two different conditions of the car’s historical price (high versus low). The participants were given three consumer reviews with reviewer-reported prices. In the condition of high historical price, the reviewer-reported prices were $29,190, $29,195, and $29,189, respectively. In the condition of low historical price, the three prices were $26,190, $26,195, and $26,189 (see the Online Appendix for description of the stimuli). Participants perceived the historical price (M= 4.76,SD=1.36) higher in the former condition than in the latter condition (M=4.16,SD=1.08,F(1,183)=11.25,p< 0.01). Among the 185 participants who completed the experiment, 104 chose to accept the offer. Logistic regression results confirmed that participants in the condition of high historical price had a higher chance of accepting the offer (67.71%) than those in low-price condition (43.82%,t= 3.35,p< 0.01). We also found evidence that consumers’ preference for the product was higher when the historical price was higher (M=2.56,SD=1.37) than that was lower (M=3.01,SD=1.31,F(1,183)=5.18,p< 0.05). Participants who faced high historical price perceived the seller to have a higher bargaining power (M=4.56,SD=1.57), whereas with low historical price, perceived the buyer to have higher bargaining power (M=3.70,SD=1.56,F(1,183)=13.98,p< 0.01). A total of 200 participants were recruited (46.81%female,Mage=36.05,SDage=11.22) for the third experiment. We randomly assigned participants to two conditions: high versus low discount frequency. In the condition of high discount frequency, we gave the participants three consumer reviews that included details on discounts offered by the dealer. In the condition of low discount frequency, the three reviews did not include any discount-related information. We describe the stimuli in the Online Appendix. After removing the incomplete cases, the data contained 188 observations. For a manipulation check, we asked the participants “Do these reviews contain discount-related information?” As expected, we successfully manipulated the two conditions (M1=1.73,SD1=0.45versusM2=1.16,SD2=0.37,F(1,186)=92.94,p< 0.01). We found that consumers who faced a low discount frequency (44.09%) were more likely to accept the offer than those who faced a high discount frequency (27.37%). This argument was supported by the logistic regression result (t= 2.41,p< 0.05). Consistent with our prediction, a one-way ANOVA showed that participants in the condition of high discount frequency (M=3.43,SD=1.45) perceived that the buyer had a higher bargaining power than the other group of participants (M=4.02,SD=1.54,F(1,186)=7.20,p< 0.01). We also found that the liking for the product was lower for participants in the condition of high discount frequency (M=2.77,SD=1.36) than those in the condition of low discount frequency (M=2.30,SD=1.04,F(1,186)=7.01,p< 0.01). We tested our hypothesis on the volume in the fourth experiment. We randomly assigned participants (45.96%female,Mage=37.44,SDage=11.79) to the high- and low-volume condition. In both conditions, we presented an aggregated consumer reviews with an average rating of 9/10. In the high-volume condition, the total number of reviews was 1,362, and in the low-volume condition, the number of reviews was 36. Participants who completed the survey were included in the analysis. As expected, participants in the high-volume condition (M=5.71,SD=0.89) perceived the number of reviews as higher than those in the low volume condition (M=5.25,SD=1.25,F(1,196)=9.06,p< 0.01). For the volume, we did not find evidence that the two groups of participants were different in terms of their willingness to accept the offer (57.00%versus56.12%,t= −0.12,p< 0.1). The one-way ANOVA indicated that the two groups of people were not significantly different in their preference for the product (M1=2.50,SD1=1.54versusM2=2.50,SD2=1.17,F(1,183)<1e−3,p< 0.1) and their bargaining power (M1=4.50,SD1=1.33versusM2=4.39,SD2=1.57,F(1,196)=0.28,p< 0.01). In this experimental study, we tested the causal links between the four review characteristics and baseline preference, perceived bargaining power, and the final transaction price. We show that consistent with previous research, review features such as average rating, historical price, and discount frequency affect baseline preference. One thing worth noticing is that laboratory results only reflect the impact of review characteristics on consumers’ perceived bargaining powers rather than realized bargaining powers in actual purchases. Results may change if there is a large deviation of the perceived bargaining power and the realized bargaining power in the automobile market. In addition, these features affect bargaining power and subsequently participants’ willingness to accept an offer. These experimental results provided support for our hypothesis that reviews might affect a consumer’s utility through two routes.
article.7. Discussion and Conclusion: It has been well documented that online reviews offer great value to modern consumers in making decisions on product purchases. Despite its importance, price negotiation has received relatively little attention in this stream of studies. Interestingly, the impact of online reviews on product choices can be complex in such a context. The reason for consumers to read online reviews goes beyond understanding the product features and quality. Consumers also use reviews to learn how much bargaining power they have to negotiate a better purchase price. We developed and estimated a structural model, in which a consumer makes decisions of product choice and price negotiation. Online reviews may affect the consumer’s baseline preference and the bargaining power. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to provide a theoretical framework to decompose the multiple impacts online reviews have on consumer demand in a negotiation context. Our theoretical framework suggests that when price is negotiable, online reviews may affect product choices via two mechanisms: the baseline preference mechanism and the bargaining power mechanism. In the baseline preference mechanism, online reviews affect a consumer’s baseline preference for a product and thereby the choice probability. In the bargaining power mechanism, reviews affect consumer choice probability through the bargaining power and the negotiated price. The bargaining power mechanism, despite its importance, has not been studied in the literature of consumer reviews. We are also the first to develop an identification strategy to separate the bargaining power mechanism and the baseline preference mechanism empirically. We leverage the information of the marginal cost to identify the bargaining power, a variable that is important but not directly observed by researchers. As the marginal cost is not observable by consumers, it does not enter a consumer’s utility function directly and affects the negotiated price only through the bargaining power. If the effect of marginal cost on the negotiated price is moderated by online reviews, then online reviews should affect the bargaining power directly. We can apply this identification strategy to a wide range of contexts involving price negotiation. Our estimation results reveal several intriguing findings. We find a double-sword-edge impact of reviews on product choices. On the one hand, good reviews raise a consumer’s baseline preference, increasing the choice probability. On the other hand, good reviews lower a consumer’s bargaining power, thereby increasing the negotiated price, which can possibly hurt choice probability.10Sellers that are unaware of this potential harm on demand may take nonoptimal strategies in reputation management. We find the average bargaining power of the Chinese automobile market is 0.484, and there is a substantial amount of heterogeneity in bargaining power for consumers who purchase different products. A consumer’s bargaining power is also affected by competition intensity in the local market. We find thathistorical priceanddiscount frequencyhave strong impacts on a consumer’s bargaining power. Specifically, a higherdiscount frequencyis correlated with a higher relative bargaining power for consumers, whereas a higherhistorical priceis associated with a higher relative bargaining power on the dealer side. This change in bargaining power leads to a change in the negotiated price paid by the consumer, which either saves or costs the consumer a sizeable amount of money. Our results offer several managerial implications to sellers and review platforms. First, our empirical analysis helps sellers better manage online WOM in the negotiation context. For example, a popular selling practice is to offer consumers discounts in exchange for positive reviews. However, our analysis suggests that this strategy can backfire when the transaction price is negotiated. If the buyer posts any price and discount information in the review, future consumers are likely to leverage this information to negotiate a low purchase price, thereby hurting seller profits. For example, our counterfactual analysis suggests that if the historical price posted in online reviews lowers by one standard deviation, seller profits can drop by22.90%. Similarly, one standard deviation increase in the discount frequency information can bring down seller profits by16.47%. Sellers should understand the tradeoff and be alert to the negative impact of such a practice. Second, by separately identifying and quantifying the importance of the bargaining power mechanism and the baseline preference mechanism, we offer sellers new perspectives for improving their economic return. This is especially important as negotiation exists in a wide range of business contexts, such as housing, automobile, and electronic appliance markets. Our research suggests that reviews convey important price information that affects the negotiation outcome, and sellers should be aware of the knowledge domain of consumers and design their bargaining tactics accordingly. For example, salespeople can leverage the high rating of a product to convince a consumer to pay a higher price for a product, as10.19%of the change in profits comes from the bargaining power mechanism. Third, our research also offers recommendations for review platforms on effective design. In the negotiation context, consumers read online reviews not only to obtain product feature related information but also to seek good deals and learn bargain tactics. Our research shows that various review features affect consumer demand by taking different mechanisms, and review platforms can use this knowledge to present review information more effectively. We find that historical price information reported by past reviewers may affect consumer bargaining power, suggesting that review platforms may consider presenting aggregate statistics on historical prices to help consumers make a more informed purchase decision. For example,Truecar.comlists this price information side by side with a car’s MSRP to facilitate consumers to achieve a more advantageous position in price negotiation. The review platform may also consider extracting and highlighting the keywords associated with the discount information in reviews to help consumers negotiate a better price. In addition, as some review features provide great value to consumers in price negotiation, a review platform may consider charging consumer fees for accessing certain review information. Our paper is not without limitations. First, although our model is flexible enough to accommodate other settings, we restrict our attention to the automobile industry. Future research could consider other durable good markets such as real estate, furniture, electronic appliances, and so forth. Second, we did not observe consumer demographics such as age and gender in our data. If researchers have available data, it would be interesting to see the segment of consumers that benefits more from reading online reviews in price negotiation. These limitations offer great opportunities for future studies, and we hope our research can stimulate more exploration of the intersection between online reviews and negotiated pricing.
